narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 59.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students, focusing on factors like early academic performance, later grades, test scores, and background that correlate with bar success. The model's judgment hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which was the strongest signal pushing toward a failure prediction, as it suggests your performance relative to peers slipped notably by that point compared to the average student's 5th decile standing. Nearly balancing that out was your strong undergraduate GPA, which had the second-biggest influence by pulling strongly in the direction of passing the bar, highlighting how your college record demonstrated real potential. Coming in third was your Year 1 decile also at 1st—again below the typical 5th or 6th decile—which nudged the prediction further toward failure, indicating early struggles that the model sees as a concerning pattern. Your LSAT score of 28, quite a bit lower than the average 37, and middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Full-time enrollment plans aligned with the vast majority of students, so that didn't sway things much either. In essence, while your undergrad success showed promise, the model weighed your weaker decile rankings in Years 1 and especially Year 3 as the dominant risks for bar failure, outweighing other strengths for someone in your demographic profile. Compared to typical admitted students, who often maintain mid-to-high deciles throughout and pair them with stronger LSATs, your profile signaled higher uncertainty despite the GPA edge. This isn't a judgment on your character or effort—it's the model prioritizing bar passage stats to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking academic coaching to improve decile rankings if reapplying elsewhere, or exploring post-bac programs to build a stronger pre-law record. Many students in similar situations rebound by targeting schools with holistic reviews or gaining practical experience first. You've already shown GPA strength—lean into that as you refine your path.
